title: Discover the Beauty of Notre Dame's Chapels
highlights: Middle Ages, wealthy families had chapels consecrated to ensure regular services given in memory of deceased relatives. To do this, the families created a foundation, offered an annuity to a chaplain, and monopolized a dedicated space. In the 14th century, the number of foundations increased, as did the number of chapels. Chapels are decorated with an altar, a light, statues or paintings, reliquaries of a patron saint, tombs, and sometimes elaborate wall decorations. Drawings show us that many elements from Notre-Dame’s chapels were destroyed during the French Revolution. Several bishops and archbishops of Paris are buried in these chapels. The decoration of the tomb of Simon Matifas de Bucy, bishop from 1290 to 1304, is the only vestige painted in the 13th century that remains in Notre-Dame Cathedral. Among

Regularly enriched since the 19th century, its presentation was revamped in 2012. ## The Chapels Twenty-nine chapels added in the 13th century surround the interior of the building. Those placed around the choir are called radiating chapels. Wishing for a regular service given in memory of a deceased person, wealthy families had chapels consecrated in the Middle Ages. title: The Lateral Chapels of Notre-Dame in Context
highlights: The Lateral Chapels of Notre-Dame in Context  ...    ## Abstract The lateral chapels appended to the cathedral of Notre-Dame of Paris starting after 1228 are the earliest dated examples of the ubiquitous standard chapel type that flourished throughout Western Europe. Although diminutive in scale and relatively simple architecturally, the chapels had a profound effect on the cathedral, altering it physically and functionally. This study examines conventional art historical sources, such as documents and the building fabric, in  ...    posits a connection between the emergence of chapels at the cathedral and the preoccupation with the nascent doctrine of purgatory in Parisian intellectual circles in the early thirteenth century. Built primarily to accommodate chantries (endowments for private masses believed to hasten the soul's release from purgatory), the chapels responded to and helped promote changing conceptions of the afterlife.
